strongest_counterevidence: Khaburzaniya, Chalkias, Lewi, Malvai, "Aggregating and thresholdizing hash-based signatures using STARKs", IACR ePrint 2021/1048 (rev. 2022-03-14), AsiaCCS 2022, DOI 10.1145/3488932.3524128 — https://eprint.iacr.org/2021/1048. Its Table 3 publishes exactly the artifact the claim says does not exist: STARK prover wall-clock time, prover peak RAM, and proof size at batch sizes n = 128 / 256 / 512 / 1024, at two security levels (96-bit and 123-bit), on explicitly named hardware ("8-core Intel Core i9 processor @ 2.4 GHz with 32 GB of RAM"), for in-circuit verification of a post-quantum (hash-based Lamport+) signature. Amortized prover cost per signature falls straight out (96-bit: 19.5 / 19.9 / 20.5 / 19.2 ms per signature at n=128/256/512/1024). It is reproducible: the AIR ships in facebook/winterfell, which independently publishes a second table (64–1024 signatures: 1.2 s / 0.5 GB / 110 KB up to 20.5 s / 7.6 GB / 152 KB, verify 4.4–5.9 ms) on a named i9-9980KH. Two more near-misses: (a) leanEthereum/leanBench (https://github.com/leanEthereum/leanBench, dashboard https://bench.leanroadmap.org/) is a live, public, seed-pinned harness measuring zkVM aggregate proving of a PQ hash-based signature at flat batch sizes 125–1000 plus tree fan-ins, results contributed as JSON per hardware fingerprint — i.e. clean reproducible amortized prover cost vs batch size, published continuously; (b) HAPPIER (Saygan, Gündoğan, Arslan, Gönen, LightSec 2025, https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-032-15541-2_1) aggregates a PQ hash-based signature (XMSS) inside RISC Zero up to 2^16 signatures on a laptop — peer-reviewed zkVM + PQ signature + batch axis. I could NOT read HAPPIER's full text (Springer auth wall), so whether it tabulates prover seconds vs batch size is UNVERIFIED and is the single largest live refutation risk. Also relevant: ZK-ACE (arXiv:2603.07974v3, https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07974) Table 7 publishes amortized STARK prover cost per transaction as an explicit function of batch size N=1..16 (14.29 → 14.77 ms/tx, Criterion, Apple M3 Pro), in the PQ-blockchain framing, though for a non-signature circuit.
what_survives: The narrow claim that survives, and the only one safe to make publicly: "No one has published measured, reproducible, same-harness prover cost per signature — wall-clock, peak RAM, and proof size on named hardware — for in-circuit verification of the three NIST-standardized signature schemes ML-DSA-44 (FIPS 204), Falcon/FN-DSA-512 (FIPS 206 draft), and SLH-DSA-128s (FIPS 205) inside a general-purpose zkVM, swept across batch size, with a classical (Ed25519/ECDSA) control in the same harness." Every qualifier there is load-bearing: (1) the three standardized schemes specifically — the entire published batch-vs-prover-cost literature uses Lamport+, XMSS, leanSig, or purpose-built SNARK-friendly schemes, never the FIPS trio; (2) prover cost, not proof size — the Falcon-in-ZK literature (LaBRADOR ePrint 2024/311, BTQ PQScale) publishes sizes only, and 2024/311's companion repo dfaranha/aggregate-falcon is an analytic size estimator with no prover at all; (3) general-purpose zkVM rather than a hand-written AIR — 2021/1048 is a bespoke AIR, leanVM is purpose-built for one signature; (4) FIPS-conformant hashing — every fast published in-circuit Falcon number relaxes the standard (Miden uses RPO-Falcon512 not FN-DSA; OpenZeppelin's own Cairo table shows the SHAKE-256-conformant variant costs 312,892 steps vs 97,681 for the Poseidon-native one, a 3.2x gap nobody states as a caveat); (5) same-harness classical control — nobody has published Falcon vs Ed25519 vs ECDSA proved by the same prover on the same machine. Additionally, do NOT frame the finding as "nobody knows how prover cost amortizes with batch size" — 2021/1048 and ZK-ACE Table 7 both already show the answer (prover time and RAM are linear in N, so amortized prover cost per signature is essentially FLAT; only proof bytes amortize). Claiming that as a discovery is the specific thing that would embarrass the founder.
reasoning:
I set refuted=true under the instruction to default that way on anything close, because the claim as worded is protected only by a thin membrane of scheme-specificity, and its most quotable implication is already published.

What I checked fresh today (2026-07-23), all zero-result: ePrint search for "zkVM post-quantum signature aggregation" (one irrelevant hit, 2026/858 on FRI soundness), "signature aggregation zkVM proving time batch" (no results), "SPHINCS+ RISC Zero SP1 proving benchmark" (no results), "Falcon Dilithium SPHINCS in-circuit verification comparison" (no results); arXiv API for all:"zkVM" AND all:"post-quantum signature" (totalResults 0), abs:"ML-DSA" AND abs:"zero-knowledge" (0), all:"post-quantum" AND all:"zkVM" (0). So the academic lens — "a 2025-2026 eprint/arXiv paper already did this" — does NOT find a paper doing the FIPS trio in a zkVM against batch size. On that literal reading the claim holds.

But three things make the public novelty claim unsafe as currently worded. First, ePrint 2021/1048 already published the exact deliverable shape (amortized STARK prover seconds + RAM + proof bytes vs batch size, named hardware, open code, peer-reviewed at AsiaCCS 2022) for a post-quantum signature — just a hash-based OTS rather than a FIPS scheme. A hostile reviewer reads the claim, finds Table 3, and the novelty collapses to "we swapped the signature." Second, leanBench is a live public harness doing continuous amortized-prover-cost-vs-batch measurement for a PQ signature across hardware, which directly contradicts the plain-English reading of "no one has published clean, reproducible numbers ... as a function of batch size." Third, HAPPIER is a peer-reviewed zkVM (RISC Zero) + PQ hash-based signature + 2^16 batch result whose full text I could not read; if it tabulates prover time vs batch size, the claim is refuted outright for the hash-based leg, and SLH-DSA-128s is the same Merkle/WOTS+ family as its XMSS, which makes the distinction hard to defend rhetorically.

Two more pressure points. The in-circuit implementations of the named schemes already exist and are partially measured: OpenZeppelin/cairo-pq-verifiers publishes five Falcon-512 Cairo variants in L2 gas and Cairo steps (97,681 to 413,819 steps) inside a STARK-provable VM with an ECDSA control, and starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow has Falcon-512 AND SPHINCS+-128s SHA2 verifiers DONE in Cairo with Stwo — with "Stwo proving benchmarks" as an explicitly unchecked roadmap item. StarkWare scoped this exact benchmark and did not publish it, which is good for novelty but also means it could land at any time and is not defensible as a moat. Meanwhile ML-DSA-44 is the genuinely emptiest cell: OpenZeppelin's ML-DSA verifier is stubbed, the only measured in-zkVM figures are two zero-star SP1 repos and one Medium post whose cycle table does not sum to its own total and whose GitHub repos 404, and ZK-ACE's widely-quoted ">= 2M R1CS constraints" is the author's own uncited structural estimate, not a measurement.

Confidence is medium, not high, for two concrete reasons: the WebSearch budget was exhausted before I could run the three fresh keyword queries the task required (I substituted structured ePrint/arXiv API queries, which have much worse recall on grey literature, blog posts, and repo READMEs — precisely where this field publishes), and HAPPIER's evaluation section remains unread behind a paywall. Before publishing any novelty claim, someone must read HAPPIER's full text and re-check s2morrow's roadmap checkbox.

strongest_counterevidence:
Two sources, both of which already publish exactly the artifact the claim says nobody has published — a clean, reproducible amortized STARK-prover-cost-vs-batch-size curve for post-quantum signature verification in-circuit:

(1) https://eprint.iacr.org/2021/1048 — Khaburzaniya, Chalkias, Lewi, Malvai, "Aggregating and thresholdizing hash-based signatures using STARKs", ASIA CCS '22, DOI 10.1145/3488932.3524128. Table 3 is literally the target deliverable: prover time, prover RAM, and proof size at batch sizes n = 128 / 256 / 512 / 1024, at two security levels, on stated hardware ("8-core Intel Core i9 @ 2.4 GHz, 32 GB RAM"). 96-bit aggregate: 2.5 s / 0.9 GB / 68 KB at n=128 up to 19.7 s / 7.4 GB / 83 KB at n=1024, i.e. ~19-20 ms of prover time per signature, flat across every batch size. It is reproducible: the code shipped as the Lamport+ aggregation example in facebook/winterfell, whose README publishes an independent second table (64→1024 sigs: 1.2 s→20.5 s proving, 0.5→7.6 GB RAM, 110→152 KB proof, 4.4→5.9 ms verify) on named hardware. That is a published, reproducible, hardware-stated, batch-size-swept amortized STARK prover cost for a hash-based PQ signature — the exact shape of the claimed gap, five years old.

(2) https://github.com/leanEthereum/leanBench (fetched 2026-07-23) — a live, running benchmark harness whose entire purpose is amortized prover cost vs batch size for PQ hash-based signature verification inside a STARK zkVM (leanVM). Confirmed from the repo page: 13 aggregate variants, flat aggregation over 125 / 250 / 500 / 1000 signatures plus tree aggregation at fan-in 2/4/8, emitting "sign / verify / aggregate timings plus CPU and memory usage" to committed per-run JSON in results/<timestamp>__<fingerprint>.json, grouped by hardware fingerprint, with a published dashboard. Reproducibility is explicitly engineered in (pinned SHAs, deterministic seed 0xC0FFEE, local `uv run bench` and remote GCP runners). Corroborated by the leanMultisig README's own figures (1,426 XMSS/s at 327 KiB proof, rate 1/2, M4 Max 48 GB) and the recursive-batch numbers (700-XMSS batches; 4 batches at rate 1/4 = 0.64 s, 105 KiB).

Secondary: ePrint 2025/055 Tables 2 and 3 (CiC Vol 2 No 1) publish the per-signature verifier hash/permutation counts — the cost driver such a benchmark measures — across the full parameter grid, and the whole leanroadmap.org research track "Post-Quantum Signature Aggregation with zkVMs" (~50% complete, evaluating Binius M3, SP1, KRU, STU, Jolt, OpenVM) is an ecosystem-scale effort doing this cross-zkVM comparison right now.
what_survives:
Narrow to the three named FIPS schemes and this survives: no one has published amortized zkVM/STARK prover cost per transaction vs batch size for ML-DSA-44 (FIPS 204), Falcon-512/FN-DSA (FIPS 206 draft), or SLH-DSA-128s (FIPS 205) specifically. I re-checked this fresh via the GitHub search API on 2026-07-23 (repository search, unauthenticated): "falcon+zkvm" → 0 repos, "sphincs+zkvm" → 0, "post-quantum+zkvm+benchmark" → 0, "pqc+sp1+prover" → 0, "dilithium+zkvm" → 1 (vjb/quantum-safe-cre, zero numbers), "ml-dsa+zkvm" → 2 (adds ebulgin/qrypta-pu: one commit, N=1, "~40 min / ~14 GB", no cycles, no batching). Nothing from RISC Zero, Succinct, Polygon, or StarkWare. Corroborating negatives already in the corpus: starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow has "Stwo proving benchmarks" as an explicitly UNCHECKED roadmap box despite having a working Falcon-512 Cairo verifier; SP1's own precompile docs list no lattice/NTT/PQ precompile; babybear-labs/benchmark and NethermindEth/zkvm-benchmarks have no PQ workload at all.

So the defensible claim is: "the existing batch-size-vs-prover-cost curves are all for non-standardized, circuit-co-designed hash-based schemes (Lamport+, leanXMSS, RPO-Falcon512); nobody has published the equivalent curve for the actual NIST-standardized schemes, and nobody has published it cross-prover." The founder must NOT say "no one has published amortized STARK prover cost per signature vs batch size" — that sentence is false and a reviewer will produce ePrint 2021/1048 Table 3 within one search.
reasoning:
The claim as written conflates two very different assertions and the weaker one is dead.

METHODOLOGICAL NOVELTY: refuted outright. "Amortized STARK prover cost per transaction for in-circuit PQ signature verification as a function of batch size" is a solved, published, reproducible measurement — done in 2021/2022 by a Meta/Polygon/UIUC team (ePrint 2021/1048, ASIA CCS '22, Table 3, n=128..1024, prover seconds + RAM + proof bytes on stated hardware), independently re-tabulated in the facebook/winterfell README, and currently being redone continuously at ecosystem scale by leanBench across 125/250/500/1000-signature flat aggregation and tree aggregation with committed per-hardware JSON. The corpus's own gap list ("NO wall-clock prover time for in-circuit Falcon in ANY system", "NO peak memory figures anywhere") is scheme-scoped and does not license the general claim; the corpus contradicts the general claim in its own hashsig-in-zk section and the founder appears not to have noticed.

Worse for the founder, 2021/1048 already reports the *headline finding* such a benchmark would produce: prover time and RAM are strictly linear in batch size, so amortized prover cost per signature does NOT improve with batching (~19-20 ms/sig at every n from 128 to 1024); only proof size amortizes (83 KB for 1024 sigs = ~83 B/sig). ZK-ACE Table 7 independently reproduces the same flat-prover/amortizing-bytes shape on a Circle STARK (14.29 → 14.77 ms/tx across N=1..16, 122 KB → 13 KB/tx). If the new benchmark's punchline is "cost per tx flattens/falls with batch size", that punchline is already published twice and the answer is known to be "no, only bytes amortize."

SCHEME-SPECIFIC NOVELTY: survives, and I tested it adversarially under the vendor lens rather than assuming. GitHub repository search on 2026-07-23 returns literally zero repos for falcon+zkvm, sphincs+zkvm, post-quantum+zkvm+benchmark, and pqc+sp1+prover, and the only ML-DSA/Dilithium zkVM repos are two hobby projects with 0 stars publishing no cycle counts. No RISC Zero, Succinct, Polygon, or StarkWare artifact publishes these numbers. StarkWare's own s2morrow scoped the Falcon proving benchmark and left the box unchecked. The only measured Falcon-in-STARK-environment data (OpenZeppelin cairo-pq-verifiers) is Cairo steps/L2 gas, not prover seconds, with ML-DSA-44 stubbed. So a FIPS-conformant, full-path, cross-prover ML-DSA-44 / Falcon-512 / SLH-DSA-128s benchmark is genuinely unoccupied.

METHOD LIMITATION I MUST DISCLOSE: the session's WebSearch budget was exhausted (200/200) before I could issue the three fresh queries the lens asked for. I substituted programmatic GitHub API repository search across six fresh query strings plus direct WebFetch verification of leanBench, which is stronger evidence for the vendor-lens question than blog-title search would have been, but it means I did NOT re-sweep vendor blogs (RISC Zero, Succinct, Polygon, StarkWare, L2 team posts) in this pass. A vendor blog post published in the last few months carrying ML-DSA-in-SP1 batch numbers would flip the surviving claim, and I cannot rule it out. That is why confidence is medium, not high — and why refuted=true is the safe call regardless: the general-form claim is already dead on the papers I did verify, so the founder is at risk either way.

strongest_counterevidence: https://eprint.iacr.org/2021/1048 — Khaburzaniya, Chalkias, Lewi, Malvai, "Aggregating and thresholdizing hash-based signatures using STARKs" (ePrint 2021/1048, revised 2022-03-14; AsiaCCS 2022, DOI 10.1145/3488932.3524128), accessed 2026-07-23. This publishes exactly the deliverable the novelty claim says nobody has published: clean, reproducible, amortized STARK prover cost for post-quantum signature verification in-circuit AS A FUNCTION OF BATCH SIZE. Table 3 gives prover time, prover RAM and proof size at n = 128 / 256 / 512 / 1024 at two security levels on named hardware ("an 8-core Intel Core i9 processor @ 2.4 GHz with 32 GB of RAM"): 96-bit aggregate = 2.5 s / 0.9 GB / 68 KB at n=128 rising to 19.7 s / 7.4 GB / 83 KB at n=1024; 123-bit = 3.2 s / 1.2 GB / 129 KB up to 25.7 s / 9.5 GB / 165 KB. Amortized prover cost per signature (my arithmetic on their table): 19.5, 19.9, 20.5, 19.2 ms/sig at n = 128, 256, 512, 1024 — i.e. FLAT. It is reproducible: the AIR ships in facebook/winterfell, whose README independently publishes the same-shaped curve (64→1024 sigs: 1.2→20.5 s proving, 0.5→7.6 GB RAM, 110→152 KB proof, 4.4→5.9 ms verify on an i9-9980HK). Secondary corroboration that the batch-size answer is already known: ZK-ACE (arXiv:2603.07974v3, Table 7, "zk-ace v0.4.0", Apple M3 Pro) measures Circle-STARK prove/tx at 14.29, 14.45, 14.46, 15.76, 14.77 ms across N = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 while proof/tx falls 122 KB → 13 KB. Two independent STARK stacks, five years apart, already publish the amortization curve and it says the same thing.
what_survives: A much narrower claim survives, and it is the only one that should be made publicly: "No one has published measured, same-harness, FIPS-conformant cycle counts and wall-clock prover cost for ML-DSA-44, Falcon-512 and SLH-DSA-128s verification inside a general-purpose RISC-V zkVM (SP1 / RISC Zero), swept across batch size, with a classical-signature control on the same machine." That survives because: (a) the only published SP1 ML-DSA cycle count (5,625,411 cycles, ML-DSA-65) is from a self-published Medium post whose own component breakdown sums to ~1.1M against a stated 5.63M total, whose GitHub repo is empty, and whose circuit uses a 4-challenge probabilistic NTT check at 60-bit soundness — not a sound verifier; (b) there is no Falcon cycle count in any RISC-V zkVM at all (NethermindEth/zkvm-benchmarks has zero hits for "falcon"; babybear-labs/benchmark has no PQ entry); (c) there is no SLH-DSA/SPHINCS+ zkVM cycle count anywhere — the SLotH figures (SLH-DSA-SHAKE-128f verify 434,660 cycles) are silicon accelerator cycles, a categorically different unit; (d) every fast published number relaxes the standard somewhere (Miden's 337,402 cycles are RPO-Falcon512, RPO256 substituted for SHAKE-256, with decompression outside the VM; OpenZeppelin's own Cairo table shows the FIPS-conformant SHAKE-256 variant costs 312,892 steps vs 97,681 for the Poseidon-native one, a 3.2x standards-compliance tax nobody else discloses). What does NOT survive is any claim that the SHAPE of the answer is unknown, or that batch-size amortization of STARK prover cost for PQ signatures is unexplored territory.
reasoning:
ADJACENT-WORK LENS VERDICT: the claim is technically true only for the exact three-scheme × RISC-V-zkVM intersection, and the interesting half of it — the batch-size amortization curve — is already published, twice, with the answer being boring.

1. THE DERIVATION IS ALGEBRAICALLY TRIVIAL. zkVM prover cost is linear in cycles above the minimum shard size, so amortized cost per transaction at batch size N is C(N)/N = c·r + F·r/N, where c = cycles per in-circuit signature verification, F = fixed harness/recursion overhead in cycles, r = $ per cycle. That is a two-parameter hyperbola. Anyone holding c and r can plot the entire "as a function of batch size" axis in a spreadsheet without running a prover. The founder's headline axis is one division.

2. AND THE CURVE'S SHAPE IS ALREADY MEASURED AND PUBLISHED. This is the killer. ePrint 2021/1048 Table 3: prover seconds and prover RAM are strictly LINEAR in n (2.5→19.7 s and 0.9→7.4 GB from n=128 to n=1024), so amortized prover ms/signature is flat at ~19-20 ms across an 8x batch range; only proof SIZE amortizes (68 KB at n=128 → 83 KB at n=1024, i.e. 531 B/sig → 83 B/sig). ZK-ACE Table 7 reproduces the identical structure on a modern Circle STARK (Stwo/M31/Poseidon2): 14.29 → 14.77 ms/tx across N=1..16, proof 122 → 13 KB/tx. Lemur's related work reports HAPPIER aggregating 2^16 XMSS signatures in RISC Zero at 2.89 MB. leanBench (github.com/leanEthereum/leanBench) is a RUNNING public harness doing precisely this workload — 13 aggregate variants, flat aggregation over 125-1000 signatures, tree aggregation at various fan-ins, results grouped by hardware fingerprint, deterministic seed 0xC0FFEE, dashboard at bench.leanroadmap.org. leanMultisig publishes 1,426 XMSS/s at 327 KiB (WHIR rate 1/2) and 1,027 XMSS/s at 220 KiB (rate 1/4) on an M4 Max, plus recursive aggregation over batches of 700. A founder claiming "no one has published amortized STARK prover cost per transaction for PQ signature verification vs batch size" will be shown 2021/1048 Table 3 and leanBench within an hour of posting.

3. WHERE THE "TRIVIALLY DERIVABLE" ARGUMENT ACTUALLY BREAKS — and this is why I say partially, not fully, refuted. The derivation needs c and r, and neither is publicly pinned for the three named schemes:
   - c: no measured, sound, FIPS-conformant in-zkVM cycle count exists for ANY of ML-DSA-44, Falcon-512, or SLH-DSA-128s (see what_survives). You cannot derive RISC-V cycles from Cairo steps or from Miden's Goldilocks-native RPO variant; arithmetization is not a unit conversion. The one SP1 ML-DSA number in circulation is arithmetically self-contradictory.
   - r: I could not verify a single published $/cycle or $/PGU rate. Every docs.succinct.xyz pricing path I tried 404'd (/docs/network/developers/pricing, /usage/pricing, /developers/gas, /developers/prices, /network/introduction, and the sp1 book markdown), so SP1 proving-gas pricing is UNVERIFIED in this session. What IS public is $/proof, not $/cycle: ethproofs.org (accessed 2026-07-23) shows an RTP cohort cost column of $0.0101 (Axiom 16x5090) and $0.0052 (AntChain OpenLabs zkDTVM 8x5090), and ethproofs.org/metrics states a $0.01 per-proof cost target, a 10s latency target, a ≥70% sub-10s reliability target, and weekly GPU hourly rental price tracking. Converting $/block-proof to $/cycle requires a cycles-per-mainnet-block figure I did not verify, and the swing is enormous: the Medium post's own self-reported $0.045 per ML-DSA proof sits 2-3 orders of magnitude away from what a naive $0.01-per-block-proof ÷ block-cycles reduction would give. So the arithmetic is trivial; the constants are not published to within ~100x. That is a real, defensible gap — but it is a gap in CONSTANTS, not in METHOD, and it is a much less exciting claim than "no one has done this."

4. HARSH BOTTOM LINE FOR THE FOUNDER. Do not claim novelty on "amortized STARK prover cost per transaction vs batch size for PQ signatures" — that is 2021/1048 (AsiaCCS 2022) plus an actively maintained Ethereum Foundation benchmark harness. Claim novelty only on the intersection: the three FIPS-standardized schemes, in general-purpose RISC-V zkVMs, at full standards-conformance (parse + decompress + SHAKE hash-to-point + verify, no Poseidon/RPO substitution), same-harness, with a classical control. And lead with the finding everyone already knows so it does not look like you missed it: prover cost per signature is essentially FLAT in batch size, only proof bytes amortize — the contribution is the constants and the conformance accounting, not the curve.

CONFIDENCE: medium. High that 2021/1048 and leanBench refute the broad framing (fetched primary numbers, two independent stacks). Medium overall because I could not verify a public $/cycle rate (all Succinct pricing URLs 404'd), and because the ePrint/Springer PDFs for HAPPIER and several ePrint papers remain unfetched in this corpus — HAPPIER (RISC Zero + XMSS, LightSec 2025) is the most likely source of a full refutation on the zkVM axis and should be obtained before publishing any novelty claim.

DEFINITIONAL ATTACK: the claim is carried almost entirely by its qualifiers, and two of them ("clean", "reproducible") are unfalsifiable — any counterexample can be waved off as "not clean enough". Strip them and the claim collapses in three separate places.

1) THE METHODOLOGY IS NOT NOVEL — IT IS FIVE YEARS OLD AND PEER-REVIEWED. Khaburzaniya, Chalkias, Lewi, Malvai, "Aggregating and thresholdizing hash-based signatures using STARKs" (https://eprint.iacr.org/2021/1048, rev. 2022-03-14, AsiaCCS 2022, DOI 10.1145/3488932.3524128; accessed 2026-07-23) publishes exactly the requested table shape: prover wall-clock, prover RAM, and proof size at batch sizes n = 128 / 256 / 512 / 1024, at two security levels, on named hardware ("8-core Intel Core i9 @ 2.4 GHz, 32 GB RAM"), for a post-quantum hash-based signature verified IN-CIRCUIT in a STARK, with the AIR open-sourced in Winterfell (https://github.com/facebook/winterfell, which republishes an independent 64/128/256/512/1024-signature table). That is clean, reproducible, amortized, and a function of batch size. It is post-quantum. It is a STARK. The ONLY thing it is not is one of the three named NIST algorithms — it uses Lamport+/Rescue-Prime, chosen precisely because it is circuit-friendly.

2) THE HEADLINE FINDING IS ALREADY KNOWN AND IS NEGATIVE. Both existing batch sweeps show amortized PROVER cost per signature is essentially FLAT, not amortizing: 2021/1048 Table 3 gives 19.5 / 19.9 / 20.5 / 19.2 ms per signature at n = 128/256/512/1024 (linear prover time, linear RAM: 0.9 -> 7.4 GB), and ZK-ACE (https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07974 v3, Table 7, accessed 2026-07-23) gives 14.29 / 14.45 / 14.46 / 15.76 / 14.77 ms/tx at N = 1/2/4/8/16. Only proof BYTES amortize. So "as a function of batch size" is close to vacuous as a novelty axis for a STARK prover — the curve is known to be a horizontal line, and what a new benchmark would actually contribute is per-scheme constant factors, which is a much smaller claim than "no one has published this".

3) A LIVE PUBLIC HARNESS ALREADY DOES BATCH-SIZE SWEEPS FOR PQ SIGNATURE AGGREGATION IN A zkVM. leanEthereum/leanBench (README fetched 2026-07-23) runs "flat aggregation over 125 / 250 / 500 / 1000 sigs, plus tree aggregation with fan-in 2 / 4 / 8", records "sign / verify / aggregate timings plus CPU and memory usage", commits per-hardware result JSON to the repo and publishes it as a site. That is a reproducible, hardware-fingerprinted, amortized-vs-batch-size PQ-signature-in-zkVM benchmark that exists today. It measures leanSig/XMSS, not ML-DSA/Falcon/SLH-DSA.

WHAT ACTUALLY SURVIVES: only the scheme restriction. And that restriction is doing suspicious work, because the field routes around those three schemes DELIBERATELY — Ethereum picked leanXMSS over Dilithium for the consensus layer (https://pq.ethereum.org/, https://leanroadmap.org/), Loquat (eprint 2024/868) and CAPSS (eprint 2025/061) exist specifically to avoid in-circuit NIST-lattice verification, and StarkWare's s2morrow (https://github.com/starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow, fetched 2026-07-23) has DONE Falcon-512 and SPHINCS+-128s Cairo verifiers but left "Stwo proving benchmarks" explicitly unchecked. A founder claiming novelty here should expect the question "if this is so obviously missing, why did three well-funded teams who built the verifiers decline to publish the proving numbers?" — the plausible answer is that the numbers are uninteresting (flat amortization, huge constants) rather than that nobody thought of it.

SECONDARY RISK: the claim is also over-narrow in a way that makes it brittle rather than impressive. "Falcon-512" already has measured in-circuit-execution numbers in a STARK-provable VM (OpenZeppelin/cairo-pq-verifiers, five variants, 97,681 to 413,819 Cairo steps, accessed 2026-07-23; Miden 337,402 cycles for RPO-Falcon512) — a hostile reader will say the prover-second/RAM axis is a mechanical extension of published step counts, not a new result.

CAVEATS ON MY OWN REFUTATION: I did not find a single source that does all of {ML-DSA-44 or Falcon-512 or SLH-DSA-128s} x {measured prover seconds and RAM} x {batch-size sweep}. My WebSearch budget was exhausted at 200 calls this session, so I could not run fresh targeted searches for a 2026 paper that might close this exactly; that residual is why confidence is medium, not high. The scheme-specific gap appears real. The claim as WORDED is refuted; the underlying engineering gap is not.
strongest_counterevidence: https://eprint.iacr.org/2021/1048 — Khaburzaniya, Chalkias, Lewi, Malvai, "Aggregating and thresholdizing hash-based signatures using STARKs" (AsiaCCS 2022, DOI 10.1145/3488932.3524128; accessed 2026-07-23). Publishes a full prover-time / prover-RAM / proof-size table at batch sizes n = 128, 256, 512, 1024 at 96-bit and 123-bit security, on named hardware (8-core Intel Core i9 @ 2.4 GHz, 32 GB RAM) — e.g. 96-bit aggregate: 2.5 s / 0.9 GB / 68 KB at n=128 rising to 19.7 s / 7.4 GB / 83 KB at n=1024 — for post-quantum hash-based (Lamport+) signature verification done IN-CIRCUIT in a STARK AIR, with the code open-sourced and independently re-benchmarked in Facebook's Winterfell (https://github.com/facebook/winterfell: 64/128/256/512/1024-signature table with trace time, proving time, RAM, proof size, verify time). Runner-up: https://github.com/leanEthereum/leanBench (fetched 2026-07-23), a live public harness sweeping flat aggregation over 125/250/500/1000 signatures with committed per-hardware result JSON and a published dashboard.
what_survives: The honest, defensible version: "No one has published measured PROVER wall-clock, peak RAM, and proof size for a standards-conformant, full-path verification of the FIPS-standardized signatures — ML-DSA-44 (FIPS 204), SLH-DSA-128s (FIPS 205), and FN-DSA-512/Falcon-512 (draft FIPS 206) — inside a general-purpose zkVM, on named hardware with a runnable artifact, swept across batch size." Three further honesty requirements, without which even this narrower claim is attackable: (a) cite eprint 2021/1048 and leanBench as the prior art that established the methodology, and frame the contribution as extending it to the NIST-standardized schemes rather than inventing the measurement; (b) state up front that amortized prover cost per signature is expected to be roughly FLAT in batch size (linear prover time and RAM, only proof bytes amortize) — otherwise the "as a function of batch size" framing over-promises; (c) disclose that no mainstream zkVM ships a lattice/NTT/Poseidon-for-Falcon precompile (SP1 v6 precompile list is SHA2/SHA3/Keccak/curve25519/k256/p256/BLS12-381/RSA/bn254), so any PQ-vs-Ed25519 comparison is unaccelerated-vs-accelerated and overstates the PQ penalty unless said explicitly. Do NOT claim novelty for "amortized STARK prover cost vs batch size" as a concept, and do NOT use the words "clean" or "reproducible" as load-bearing qualifiers — reviewers will read them as an escape hatch.
